records.definition: The governed platform that supplies durable knowledge, workflow, review, authority, strategy, explainability, and auditability around replaceable reasoning models.
Domain-agnostic platform capabilities and contracts beneath Domain Packages.
A portable domain-specific capability package operating on DIP Core contracts.
A governed operating context within a Domain Package. Real instances, identifiers, memberships, and bindings belong to Private Authority.
An abstract platform capability for governed private strategic context beneath a Workspace; public material must not reveal whether any real instance exists.
A replaceable model provider or model used beneath the AI Operating Profile.
The provider-neutral constitutional behavior expected of any AI interacting with DIP.
The approved framing of DIP as the governed operating environment around replaceable AI reasoning.
The common governance and review authority presented consistently through authorized interfaces.
A minimal operational interface that is a peer to ChatGPT at the Review Service boundary.
A stable governed information identity with one or more immutable versions.
An immutable version of an Artifact, addressable independently from its stable Artifact identity.
An artifact class representing received source material before normalization or canonical acceptance.
An artifact class transformed into governed structural form without yet becoming the canonical representation.
An accepted authoritative representation within its declared authority and scope.
An artifact produced from one or more exact input versions through declared lineage.
The initiating record in the durable execution hierarchy.
A governed execution instance initiated by a Trigger and decomposed into Stages.
A durable phase of a Workflow containing one or more Jobs.
A durable executable unit within a Stage, realized through one or more Attempts.
A single execution attempt for a Job, retaining retries and outcomes without overwriting history.
A governed advisory output that cannot itself authorize mutation.
The explicit governed record of a human or authorized decision regarding a Recommendation.
An authorized instruction created only after applicable review and decision requirements are satisfied.
The recorded result of executing or attempting an authorized Command.
Architecture content intentionally approved for public publication and safe for an explicit public-source allowlist.
Non-public architectural detail retained in a separately authenticated location outside the public repository.
The authority plane owning real Workspace and Private Strategy instances, memberships, bindings, identifiers, and active private configuration.
Sensitive values that must never appear in documentation, repositories, prompts, logs, fixtures, generated files, or archives.
The minimum coherent capability profile approved for an operational DIP deployment.
An evidence-dependent control gate for a publication phase; planning completion alone does not pass the gate.
The separate human authorization boundary that permits DIP product implementation coding only after its approved publication, release, pinning, location, and authority prerequisites are satisfied.
A durable record for a decision that warrants formal architectural rationale and consequences; not every approved decision is retroactively converted into an ADR.
The application-owned transaction boundary for one command or workflow transition.
An application- or Core-owned contract through which external capability is requested without depending on infrastructure.
An infrastructure implementation of a Port, including connector, persistence, model, interface, and external-effect adapters.
An immutable compiled execution scope carrying actor, Workspace, applicable Private Strategy, authority, causation, and pinned configuration and capability versions.
A versioned Domain Package declaration of supported analyzers, workflows, schemas, review kinds, connector mappings, and commands.
An external provider or system from which DIP acquires information or to which an authorized effect may be directed.
A governed configuration and authority binding between DIP and a Source System.
A provider-facing adapter that retrieves or transmits provider-native information without owning domain normalization or governance decisions.
A separately registered read or write capability exposed by a Connector with explicit scope and authorization.
A durable request to obtain observations from one Source Connection under declared scope, freshness, and authority.
A durable grouping of related acquisition work across endpoints, with completeness and coherence metadata.
The durable record of one source observation, including provenance and payload identity, created even when payload bytes deduplicate.
The provider-side object or endpoint addressed by an authorized external Command.
A provider-relative incremental-acquisition position advanced only after supporting raw evidence is durably stored.
A durable event-publication pattern that records state change and pending event in the same Unit of Work.
A monotonically ordered claim token used to prevent a stale worker or lease holder from committing later execution state.
The property that repeated delivery or invocation produces no additional authoritative effect beyond the intended single logical operation.
A stable read-oriented MCP representation of governed DIP context.
A parameterized MCP operation routed through governed application commands or queries under explicit effect classification.
The governed plan defining one AI task, its policy, inputs, budgets, model profile, and required outputs.
An immutable record of the exact governed context assembled for one AI execution.
A versioned governed artifact containing the task-specific instructions and output contract supplied through the Model Gateway.
The durable record of one request submitted to a qualified model deployment.
The provider result received for one Model Invocation before local acceptance decisions.
A structured model-produced candidate awaiting local validation, conformance evaluation, and artifact acceptance.
The durable outcome of evaluating an AI execution or deployment against applicable AIOP and task requirements.
The provider-neutral application boundary through which DIP invokes qualified model deployments.
A logical governed selector for a qualified model deployment, routing rules, budgets, and provider configuration.
The governed application boundary exposing allowlisted query capabilities to models without direct infrastructure access.
The complete provider, model, parameters, policy, and adapter configuration qualified for a declared task.
A domain-neutral review record that binds one exact immutable proposal version, authority scope, state, and allowable actions.
A scoped projection of Review Items for an authorized review purpose or information boundary.
An immutable version of content offered for governed review.
A new Proposal Version that supersedes earlier reviewed content without mutating its history.
A grouping identity for individually authorized review decisions performed through one bulk interaction.
A stable navigation reference to an authorized interface resource that carries no authorization credential.
A human identity independent from authentication mechanism, runtime principal, or executing actor.
An authenticated security identity evaluated for access and policy decisions.
The identity attributed to a governed action, preserving both human initiator and executing mechanism where applicable.
The authorized client application or interface through which a Principal invokes DIP.
A governed association between a Person or Principal and a Workspace or other authority scope.
An explicit authorization assignment permitting declared actions over declared resources and scope.
A governed transfer of bounded authority from one authorized identity to another.
A non-human identity assigned to a scheduler, worker, migration, backup, or other runtime workload.
The external identity protocol selected for human authentication using Authorization Code with PKCE.
An opaque server-side authenticated session referenced by secure browser cookies.
PostgreSQL policy enforcement used as defense in depth for production scope-sensitive tables.
The append-oriented, content-minimized security record stream distinct from diagnostic logs and domain events.
A temporary, exceptional, explicitly audited emergency-authority workflow.
A key used to encrypt restricted payload data within a declared security realm.
A cryptographic and authorization boundary within which keys and restricted-content deduplication are scoped.
A requirement for recent or stronger authentication before a high-risk action.
The application-security verification baseline for which the production application targets the current Level 2 posture.
A distinct level of verification with declared scope, dependencies, speed, and failure-detection purpose.
An executable verification that an implementation conforms to an authoritative schema, Port, or shared semantic contract.
A test that generates input cases to verify invariants across a broad state space.
A scheduled or on-demand governed Workflow that evaluates operational state against approved architecture and policy.
A versioned evidence-bearing result of a conformance evaluation, including severity and activation implications.
A typed telemetry event emitted with controlled fields rather than free-form private content.
A bounded numeric telemetry series whose labels exclude high-cardinality domain and security identities.
A correlated telemetry representation of work across interfaces, workflows, adapters, models, and messaging boundaries.
A propagated identifier linking related operational activity without becoming authoritative domain identity.
Whether a process is running sufficiently to be restarted or supervised.
Whether a process is prepared to receive its declared work safely.
The operational availability and fitness of a required external dependency.
Whether governed information is sufficiently current for its declared product use.
A visible terminal holding state for irrecoverable or unsafe work requiring explicit operator action.
A dependency- or capability-scoped control that stops repeated unsafe calls after qualifying failures.
A bounded control that slows or rejects incoming work when safe processing capacity is exhausted.
A measurable operational objective for availability, correctness, freshness, isolation, or recovery.
An operator procedure for identifying, containing, recovering from, and documenting a declared incident class.
The immutable container artifact used to package the production application.
The content-derived immutable identity of a specific OCI Image.
An immutable record binding a deployment to image, release, configuration, migration, environment, and approval identity.
The approved version identity format for application releases and immutable release tags.
The governed action that makes a deployed capability, model, AIOP, configuration, or external effect available for use.
Database recovery to a selected recent point using retained transaction history.
The maximum acceptable amount of data loss measured backward from an incident.
The target elapsed time to restore an acceptable operational service after an incident.
A rehearsed restoration of database, payload, key, configuration, and operational state in an isolated environment.
A governed policy assigning information a retention duration, backup behavior, and deletion conditions.
An evidence-dependent implementation-sequence gate, distinct from a Publication Gate and never passed by planning alone.
A narrow end-to-end implementation that exercises the applicable governing architecture through a coherent user-visible outcome.
The formal evidence review and Decision Record required before production launch.
The evidence threshold required before value-dependent recommendations may be activated.
A single implementation repository with explicit package, application, authority, and dependency boundaries.
An application entry point that assembles dependencies and capabilities without owning reusable canonical logic.
The public engineering source set sufficient to reproduce the platform without containing real private authority or secrets.
An external governed source holding real Workspace or Private Strategy authority outside the public repository.
A reproducible derivative created from an authoritative source and subject to drift verification.
The governed catalog of authoritative schema identities, versions, compatibility, and resolution metadata.
The selected primary Python implementation and runtime family for DIP.
The reproducible multi-package Python project governed from a virtual repository root and committed lockfile.
The committed dependency-resolution record used to reproduce an approved package environment.
A validated representation used at a system edge without becoming canonical domain authority.
A framework-independent representation of Core or domain behavior, identity, and invariants.
The persistence authority that governs accepted transactional state for a declared scope.
The time-ordered UUID format selected for new DIP-generated internal identities.
The persistent identity of a governed entity across its immutable versions.
The immutable identity of one exact accepted version of a governed entity or artifact.
The time at which DIP durably recorded a fact or record.
The time at which a source observation was made or claimed.
The domain interval during which a fact applies.
The time at which a governed version became accepted authority.
The append-only transition-evidence record that does not require full event-sourced state reconstruction.
A rebuildable read or interaction model derived from authoritative records.
The storage abstraction for large or binary immutable payload objects referenced by governed records.
A digest-derived payload locator used for integrity and deduplication without becoming artifact identity.
A stored payload object that has no authoritative accepted database reference and may be safely reclaimed.
The explicit model of configuration ownership, override permission, combination, and capability ceilings across authority planes.
The machine-readable definition of field ownership and permitted configuration combination behavior.
A compiled, canonical, fingerprinted configuration result produced from governed source documents.
The governed historical record that makes one compiled Configuration Bundle active for declared scope.
A deterministic fingerprint of the canonical semantic content of a compiled configuration bundle.
The declared rule for replacing, restricting, deleting, or otherwise combining a specific configuration field.
A typed non-secret identifier that allows an approved resolver to obtain secret material at a capability boundary.
A dedicated infrastructure adapter that resolves a Secret Reference without placing secret values into authoritative configuration.
The earlier dynasty-specific name and framing retained only where historically meaningful; Domain Intelligence Platform is the current canonical name.
The canonical current name for the approved review covering the minimum viable profile, costs, vendor categories, runtime posture, scope, and readiness gates.
The approved ordering that distinguishes accepted or approved authority, explicit approval pairs, authoritative summaries, historical discussion, and prohibited inference.
The retained original decision language or faithful proposal-and-approval pair supporting a normalized publication record.
Concise publication-ready architectural wording that preserves the meaning, force, lifecycle, activation, and scope of its approved source statement.
The publication inventory containing only statements whose approval authority is demonstrable under the E1–E5 evidence hierarchy.
The explanatory narrative of how DIP evolved from its initial problem through generalization, architecture, operational narrowing, AI governance, implementation planning, and publication.
The precise traceable record of what was decided, why, when, under what authority, and what later replaced it.
The approved P0 exit gate into full Architectural Foundations drafting; it passes only when its inventory, evidence, classification, gap, and contradiction criteria are satisfied.
A controlled record of missing, incomplete, or uncertain source evidence that must remain visible rather than be reconstructed from assumption.
A controlled record of a possible incompatibility requiring review; a genuine contradiction blocks the affected publication area and requires formal architectural resolution.
A durable, accessible account of why DIP exists, how its governing ideas evolved, and what the architecture is designed to protect.
A version-specific first-person stewardship document that interprets an architecture release without creating independent normative requirements.
The modular authoritative integrated description of current DIP architecture, including normative requirements, status, conformance, and traceability.
A controlled classification that distinguishes requirement, prohibition, recommendation, permission, rationale, example, and status content.
A declared set of architecture obligations and evidence requirements applicable to a platform, package, Workspace class, runtime, slice, or portability boundary.
An authoritative non-ADR decision with stable identity and traceability whose scope does not require a full architectural alternatives-and-consequences analysis.
Creation of a formal decision record after the original decision, with the decision date and reconstruction date preserved separately.
A relationship in which a later approved decision replaces the earlier decision across its complete governing scope.
A relationship in which a later decision replaces only an explicit portion of an earlier decision while the unaffected scope remains active.
The first immutable approved DIP governing-architecture baseline; it is not a claim that implementation or production certification is complete.
A content-complete architecture candidate undergoing review that remains non-authoritative and does not authorize implementation kickoff.
The structurally complete public architecture edition built exclusively from allowlisted public sources and containing no restricted or live authority information.
The access-controlled operational stewardship publication that includes restricted architectural supplements but excludes live Private Authority state and all Secrets.
An authoritative editable Markdown, metadata, contract, or diagram source from which publication derivatives are generated.
A DOCX, PDF, compiled document, table, diagram derivative, or archive produced from canonical sources and never edited as an independent authority.
A classification-specific publication output assembled from an explicit permitted source set.
The requirement that every offline archive begin with self-contained instructions for identity, contents, validation, reconstruction, and safe use.
The property that public architecture neither confirms nor enables enumeration of any real private instance, membership, binding, identifier, or restricted artifact subject.
A source file whose content belongs entirely to one approved security classification so builds do not depend on paragraph-level redaction.
The version-pinned, scope-bounded package that translates approved architecture into authorized work, sequencing, evidence, gates, and escalation procedures.
The machine-readable identity of the exact architecture, registers, contracts, backlog, gate versions, classifications, and approval record governing implementation.
The overarching IKG0–IKG6 sequence that organizes specialized approved gates without replacing their identifiers or authority.
The end-to-end evidence standard proving the governed lifecycle through the narrow first implementation use case.
A controlled category of structural, behavioral, data, security, reliability, operational, AI-governance, portability, or human-acceptance proof.
The G0 conceptual layer defining DIP mission, hierarchy, reasoning authorities, information lifecycle, workflow, artifacts, interaction, and governance.
The ordered progression Receive → Validate → Accept → Version → Propagate → Observe.
The ordered execution identity hierarchy Trigger → Workflow → Stage → Job → Attempt.
One of the governed Domain Event, Orchestration Signal, Command, or Outcome semantic categories.
One of the Raw, Normalized, Canonical, or Derived information representations.
The controlled progression Recommendation → Decision Record → Command → Outcome.
The single accountable subsystem that owns the accepted meaning and durable transition rules for an information category.
The one logical authority responsible for committing a durable state transition before propagation.
Interpretation that preserves both when information was recorded and when it was effective.
Use managed commodity infrastructure by default while retaining DIP-controlled semantics, portability, and evidence-based self-hosting exceptions.
Evidence-based evaluation of architecture, security, recovery, portability, cost, and operator burden before selection.
The historical G0 completion state confirming that conceptual architecture and governance were approved; distinct from publication gate PG0.
The approved operational architecture applying DIP to the first Domain Package without narrowing governing architecture.
The smallest coherent domain-specific set of contracts, sources, workflows, knowledge, intelligence, and interfaces that conforms to DIP.
The approved user and operational purpose against which first-domain scope and usefulness are evaluated.
An approved end-to-end user outcome used to bound the first useful operating profile.
The first-domain mapping of facts and transitions to DIP authorities, external evidence sources, and resolution paths.
The first-domain rules for acquiring, validating, refreshing, attributing, and reconciling external evidence.
The restricted-identity boundary at which the first real operational Workspace may be activated after its approved gate.
The restricted-identity boundary used to verify Workspace portability without Core or Domain Package forking.
The AIOP role in which governed AI improves human judgment and agency through evidence, challenge, explanation, and recommendation without displacing authority.
The suitability of evidence for a decision based on provenance, freshness, applicability, quality, and relevant limitations.
Confidence in the fitness and support supplied by the available evidence.
Confidence that an inference follows from the evaluated evidence and stated assumptions.
Confidence that a recommendation or decision is appropriate after evidence, inference, goals, tradeoffs, authority, and risk are considered.
Authorized transient information assembled for an active governed task but not accepted as durable memory.
Information proposed for durable memory that has not yet passed governed review and acceptance.
Durable memory accepted through governed review, classification, scope, and authority controls.
Task context whose inclusion is authorized by actor, scope, information realm, purpose, permissions, and policy.
An AIOP classification of AI activity such as Observe, Analyze, Propose, Prepare, or Execute, each with distinct authority requirements.
The rule that authorized interfaces preserve equivalent governance, authority, review, status, and audit semantics.
A material failure represented as explicit platform state rather than hidden substitution, suppression, or unsupported success.
Learning or adaptation processed as explicit, attributable, reviewable, versioned change rather than silent behavioral mutation.
The AIOP property that foundation models and providers remain replaceable components beneath stable governance contracts.
Qualification of a complete versioned provider, model, adapter, AIOP, prompt, tool, schema, privacy, and runtime combination for defined tasks.
The 22 binding highest-order principles governing AI behavior, authority, evidence, boundaries, failure, learning, portability, and outcomes in DIP.
Alignment to authorized human and Workspace goals while preserving evidence-based challenge, uncertainty, and non-deceptive dissent.

The approved DIP framing: a governed durable operating environment around replaceable reasoning models that supplies knowledge, memory, authority, workflows, review, strategy, explainability, controlled execution, and auditability.
The durable DIP capabilities and authorities that support governed reasoning independently of a particular foundation model or provider.
An AI execution role that reasons and interacts through DIP contracts without becoming the system of record or acquiring implicit authority.
The approved public repository, named dip-architecture, that becomes authoritative for allowlisted canonical architecture publication sources after owner and immutable revision binding.
A separately authenticated repository for restricted architectural evidence and metadata; it is neither Private Authority nor a secret store.

A restricted metadata registry keyed by existing DIP-SRC identifiers that resolves authorized evidence locations, revisions, hashes, approvals, access controls, retention, and verification state without exposing restricted content publicly.
A stable hash-bearing reference that binds an explicit human approval message to its controlled source identifier and immutable evidence revision.

The public-safe representation of one approved ADR that preserves its stable identity, status, abstract decision, and public consequences while excluding restricted evidence and protected architectural detail.

The complete normalized representation of the same approved ADR retained within the separately authenticated restricted architecture boundary, while still excluding Private Authority data and secrets.
A schema-valid, semantically reviewed, public-safe machine-readable representation of a canonical DIP register that contains only explicitly approved projection fields and public relationships.
The controlled method assigned to a public register projection: deterministic field projection, field projection with semantic review, or authored public projection.
